elements.sent-2411-001: The challenge in LLM-based video understanding lies in preserving visual and semantic information in long videos while maintaining a memory-affordable token count.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, redundancy and correspondence in videos have hindered the performance potential of existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-003: Through statistical learning on current datasets, we observe that redundancy occurs in both repeated and answer-irrelevant frames, and the corresponding frames vary with different questions.
elements.sent-2411-004: This suggests the possibility of adopting dynamic encoding to balance detailed video information preservation with token budget reduction.
elements.sent-2411-005: To this end, we propose a dynamic cooperative network, DynFocus, for memory-efficient video encoding in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, i) a Dynamic Event Prototype Estimation (DPE) module to dynamically select meaningful frames for question answering; (ii) a Compact Cooperative Encoding (CCE) module that encodes meaningful frames with detailed visual appearance and the remaining frames with sketchy perception separately.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate our method on five publicly available benchmarks, and experimental results consistently demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance.
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfloat[Redundancy]{ \includegraphics[scale=0.45]{redundancy.pdf}} \subfloat[Correspondence]{ \includegraphics[scale=0.32]{intro2.pdf}} \caption{ Concept of redundancy and correspondence in our pipeline. (a) The proportion of redundancy for video datasets\protect\footnotemark. Redundancy includes both repeated and answer-irrelevant frames. Repeatance gauges the redundancy between consecutive frames, while answer-irrelevance refers to frames with a marginal contribution to question answering. (b) An example of correspondence. Given a video, we highlight the corresponding question/answer pairs and frames using red and blue boxes, respectively. } \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-008: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown their ability on general AI ~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2001-08361 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Vision Language Models (VLMs) extend the capabilities of LLMs to process visual data, demonstrating proficiency in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, challenges arise in video understanding, especially with long-term videos, where representing consecutive video frames requires an excessive number of tokens, leading to high memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent attempts use average pooling, attention, or dynamic masking to reduce video tokens spatially~\cite{li2023llama, maaz2023video, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2405-07798, jin2023chat, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-00308 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, redundant frames lead to the neglect of key visual details.
elements.sent-2411-013: Several works~\cite{DBLP:conf/cvpr/0004LJJCSSL24, videollamb capture visual appearance with memory banks to preserve key details.}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, the key details vary in correspondence to questions, which can easily result in the loss of keyframes from long videos and increase the overhead of the memory banks.
elements.sent-2411-015: In Fig.~\ref{fig:intro , we illustrate examples of redundancy and correspondence in videos.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We observe that i) there is significant redundancy among frames, with only a few meaningful frames directly contributing to question answering.
elements.sent-2411-017: This suggests the potential for adopting a dynamic frame encoding strategy to reduce tokens based on their contribution.
elements.sent-2411-018: Therefore, dynamically identifying meaningful frames offers better flexibility for sophisticated video content understanding.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this paper, we propose a dynamic cooperative framework, DynFocus, for memory-efficient video encoding.
elements.sent-2411-020: Specifically, it consists of two key modules: {Dynamic Event Prototype Estimation (DPE) and {Compact Cooperative Encoding (CCE).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: DPE serves as the dynamic selector to accurately discern the meaningful frames, which takes both redundancy and correspondence to question answering into consideration as the selection standard.
elements.sent-2411-022: Afterwards, CCE complies with the dynamic encoding principle.
elements.sent-2411-023: The meaningful frames are encoded with fine-grained context features for detailed visual appearance, whereas those redundant frames are substantially encapsulated into a few tokens for sketchy perception, which enables LLM to capture broader temporal clues within a fixed receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-024: These two modules reconcile the nuanced visual-semantic understanding with affordable token quota.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{lidar.pdf} \caption{Competitive even SOTA performance on various video understanding tasks.} \label{fig:lidar}
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, our CCE module draws inspiration from the cooperation of retinal ganglion cells in the primate visual system.
elements.sent-2411-026: Biological studies~\cite{nature, visual-iovs found that in these cells, Rod cells perceive the overall scene in a wide field of view, while Cone cells understand complex scenes with fine details.}
elements.sent-2411-027: These cells are located at the periphery of the retina and arranged in a parallel manner, receiving the signals but activated under different conditions.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our framework is analogous in two aspects: (1) Which cell is activated depends on whether the current input frame is meaningful or not.
elements.sent-2411-029: We hope these relations will further support the design philosophy of our method and reveal its rationality.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose a dynamic cooperative network, $DynFocus$, towards memory-efficient video encoding within LLM, inspired by the biological concept of $Cone$ and $Rod$ cells.
elements.sent-2411-032: We introduce two modules, DPE and CCE, that dynamically balance subtle visual appearance with sketchy temporal perception using affordable tokens.
elements.sent-2411-033: Experimentally, we evaluate our $DynFocus$ on two publicly mainstream short video benchmarks, three long video benchmarks, and one diagnosis benchmark on video hallucination as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:lidar .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In recent years, Vision Language Models (VLMs) has emerged to extend the capabilities of LLMs~\cite{radford2018improving, chowdhery2023palm, touvron2023llama, vicuna2023, openai2022chatgpt to handle diverse and complicated inputs with satisfactory generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Generally, VLMs incorporate additional connector to bridge the semantic gap between input video content and LLMs, further performing modality alignment and instruction tuning on video-based dataset.
elements.sent-2411-037: However, video understanding presents the significant challenges due to their extensive memory overhead.
elements.sent-2411-038: Several studies have dedicated to addressing these challenges with greater efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-039: Video-ChatGPT~\cite{maaz2023video adopts both spatial and temporal pooling to condense video tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-040: VideoChat~\cite{li2023videochat employs a learnable Q-former~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/Dai0LTZW0FH23 to aggregate the similar tokens for memory reduction.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Chat-UniVi~\cite{jin2023chat develops a unfied framework for processing both image and video, which reduces spatial and temporal tokens through multi-stage token merging.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Although these methods alleviate the memory usage to some extent, they often discard the abundant temporal clues by sampling parts of frames as the input.
elements.sent-2411-043: To compensate the loss of temporal clues, LLaMA-VID~\cite{li2023llama innovates with a dual-token approach that represents each frame with context and content tokens, which allows for larger video throughput.}
elements.sent-2411-044: MovieChat~\cite{Song2023MovieChatFD incorporates the short-term memory and long-term memory into unified framework, strategically combining similar frames to reduce memory footprint while capture the temporal clues.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Similarly, MA-LLM~\cite{he2024malmm stores past video information in a memory bank, which allows to reference historical video content for long-term analysis without exceeding memory limits.}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, these methods exhibit proficiency in capturing temporal clues at the expense of discarding the visual details.
elements.sent-2411-047: In a nutshell, they struggle to jointly capture the spatial details and temporal dynamics effectively.
elements.sent-2411-048: Dynamic networks, adjusting the encoding strategy according to specific input, have recently garnered the burgeoning interest across various domains.
elements.sent-2411-049: Early methods mainly focus on traditional image classification by channel pruning or layer skipping.
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, BlockDrop~\cite{DBLP:conf/cvpr/WuNKRDGF18 designed an auxiliary policy network to determine whether skip or execute convolutional blocks via reinforcement optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Based on dynamic mechanism, a series of research efforts are devoted to better adapting to the various dynamic scenes.
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, Dynamic~\cite{DBLP:conf/cvpr/LiSCLZWS20 proposes a routing network with soft conditional gate to adaptively search data-dependent scale transformation paths for semantic segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In the field of image question answering, SUPER~\cite{DBLP:journals/tip/HanYWWN23 develops a semantic-aware modular routing framework to recursively handle different complexity of visual scene.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In this work, we marks the first attempt to reveal the substantial potential of dynamic encoding strategies when understanding the complicated long-term video.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.96\linewidth]{framework.pdf} \caption{Schematic Illustration of DynFocus. Our method takes the user instruction and video frames as input, and yields the compact video tokens from CCE module for LLM. Specifically, DPE module serves as the selector to identify the prototypes that contribute greatly to answer, providing CCE module with event prototype $\{\mathbf{h}_{k}\}_{k=1}^{K}$ and the binary mask $\{b_{t}\}_{t=1}^{T}$, which is marked with two red arrows. Benefited from this, CCE module dynamically encode the critical prototypes with more tokens, and encapsulate the marginal prototypes with few tokens. T-DPC and S-DPC represent the DPC-KNN clustering temporally and spatially, respectively.} \label{fig:framework}

elements.sent-2411-209: In this section, we provide the details about how to estimate the ratio of temporal repetitive frames and answer-irrelevant frames, which is denoted as $r_{d}$ and $r_{a}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-210: Specifically, given a $T$-frame video, we use CLIP-ViT~\cite{DBLP:conf/icml/RadfordKHRGASAM21 to extract the representation for each video frame and its text part.}
elements.sent-2411-211: For temporal repetitive frames, we calculate the cosine similarities of features between consecutive frames, denoted as $s_{t = \mathrm{cos (\mathbf{f _{t , \mathbf{f _{t+1 )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: We then collect all scores into a score vector $\mathbf{s = \{s_{t}\}_{t=1 ^{T-1}$ and apply min-max normalization.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: This process can be summarized as, \begin{equation}r_{d} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} I(s_{t}>0.6)}{T-1}, \label{eqn:rd}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{I (\cdot)$ is the indicator function, defined as $\mathbf{I (\mathbf{x ) = 1$ if $\mathbf{x}$ is true, and $\mathbf{I (\mathbf{x ) = 0$ if $\mathbf{x}$ is false.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: For answer-irrelevant frames, we compute their similarity using $s_{t = \mathrm{cos (\mathbf{f _{t , \mathbf{q ||\mathbf{a )$, where $\mathbf{q || \mathbf{a}$ represents the token-wise concatenation of question and answer feature.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: After applying min-max normalization, we mark a frame as redundant when its frame-to-text similarity falls below a certain threshold.
elements.sent-2411-216: This is summarized as, \begin{equation}r_{a} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}I(s_{t}<0.4)}{T}. \label{eqn:ra}\end{equation} Notably, for each benchmark dataset, we randomly sample 20 videos to calculate the average value of redundancy ratio $r_{d}$ and $r_{a}$ as a rough redundancy estimation.

elements.subsection-2411-012: Comparison of Method Design with other Methods.
elements.sent-2411-233: In this section, we compare the design details with two closely related studies: LLaMA-VID and Chat-Univ.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our compression design in $Rods$ is somewhat similar to LLaMA-VID.
elements.sent-2411-235: However, the main difference lies in the resolution of input visual signals processed by the text-guided compression module (i.e., $Context Attention$).
elements.sent-2411-236: Specifically, LLaMA-VID directly use visual feature at their original resolution.
elements.sent-2411-237: In contrast, our method uses the generated semantic prototypes as the input of $Rods$.
elements.sent-2411-238: These prototypes are generated by merging the patch feature with different weight $\rho_{i \cdot \delta_{i}$, where $i$ denotes the patch index in single frame.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Chat-Univ adopts DPC-KNN clustering algorithm to form clusters both spatially and temporally.
elements.sent-2411-240: Our method differs from Chat-Univ in the following aspects during the clustering process: (1) Temporally: We cluster the frames by calculating the similarity using downsampled features to model more fine-grained temporal relationship, rather than using the feature after global average pooling as in Chat-Univ.
elements.sent-2411-241: This effectively avoids the information loss when performing clustering.
elements.sent-2411-242: Essentially, our model highlights adopting the dynamic encoding, which not only reduces the visual nuisance but also effectively reconciles the spatial details with temporal clues using affordable tokens.
elements.sent-2411-243: There are multiple clustering algorithm~\cite{macqueen1967some, DBLP:journals/pami/HuangNRL05 available to form the spatial and temporal prototype.}
elements.sent-2411-244: To assess the effect of different clustering on model performance, we report the results on two traditional clustering algorithms, $K$-means and weighted $K$-means in Table~\ref{tab:cluster .}
elements.sent-2411-245: To save the time overhead, we train our model using only the video-based dataset.

elements.subsection-2411-015: The Effect of Different Training Datasets
elements.sent-2411-247: In this section, we delve into the effect of data scaling on our model.
elements.sent-2411-248: We begin with adopting the only video-based dataset for training.
elements.sent-2411-249: Specifically, we use WebVid-Cap for vision-language alignment in the first stage and VideoChatGPT-100K for instruction tuning in the second stage.
elements.sent-2411-250: Compared with two strong baselines, our model scores 67.9\% on MSVD-QA, even outperforming several models that uses additional image-based dataset for training.
elements.sent-2411-251: As we introduce more image-based dataset, our method consistently shows improving performance, maintaining its leading position.
elements.sent-2411-252: Notably, the addition of CLEVRER appears to degrade the model performance.
elements.sent-2411-253: This possibly because that the visual scene involved in CLEVRER differs significantly from those in the targeted evaluation benchmarks, despite it potentially enhances the spatial reasoning and counting abilities of our model.

elements.sent-2411-254: We have two observations for longer videos: (a) the optimal $L$ shifts progressively to the right, from 30 to 55, and further to 60; (b) a smaller $\frac{K {L}$ yields better performance.}
elements.sent-2411-255: This is primarily due to long videos introducing more redundant visual events, while a smaller portion of events should be adaptively selected for question answering.
elements.sent-2411-256: The default parameters towards $L$ and $\frac{K {L}$ are set to 25 and 0.8 in the main paper when performing evaluation without specification, to achieve a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Several researches have pointed that existing MLLMs suffers from the issues of hallucination, which means that they tend to generate irrelevant or nonsensical content that deviates from the original visual context.
elements.sent-2411-258: To comprehensively demonstrate the robustness of our method, we compare the extent of video hallucination of our method with existing video MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-259: The evaluated benchmark VideoHallucer categorizes hallucinations into two main types: intrinsic and extrinsic, offering further subcategories for detailed analysis, including object-relation, temporal, semantic detail, extrinsic factual, and extrinsic non-factual hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-260: The overall results are delineated in Table~\ref{tab:hallu .}
elements.sent-2411-261: We have several following observations: (1) Although all models demonstrate strong capabilities in answering basic questions, they experience a significant decline in accuracy when dealing with hallucinated questions.
elements.sent-2411-262: This huge gap implies a widespread conclusion that existing models are vulnerable to the ``Yes/NO' 'bias.
elements.sent-2411-263: In other words, most models tend to generate the ``Yes'' answers.
elements.sent-2411-264: VideoChat2 and PLLaVA share the same video-based instructional data but obtain the diametrical results, and the difference stems from source of image-based knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-265: Specifically, the image-based knowledge preserved in PLLaVA originates from a pre-existing image-based MLLM, whereas the knowledge in VideoChat2 is learned from scratch based on collected image QA pairs.
elements.sent-2411-266: On contrary, our model achieves a clear-cut performance gain of 28.3\% compared with VideoChat2, and comparable results to PLLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-267: It is noteworthy that our method employs a dynamic encoding strategy, where each frame is encoded with 40 tokens or 2 tokens depending on its contribution to question answering, which is much less than VideoChat2 and PLLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-268: For most of input videos, we sample the frame at 1 fps following LLaVA-VID~\cite{li2023llama and Flash-Vstream~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2406-08085 , except excessive long video.}}
elements.sent-2411-269: All input images or frames are resized to 224 $\times$ 224 and encoded as 16 $\times$ 16 visual features via pre-trained EVA-G~\cite{DBLP:conf/cvpr/FangWXSWW0WC23 , and the hidden dimension $d$ is 1408.}
elements.sent-2411-270: We set $I=22$, $J=2$, $P=16$, $K=20$, and $L=25$ when training to achieve a trade-off between performance and memory efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-271: During vision-language alignment, we pre-train our model with a batch size of 256, employing AdamW~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/KingmaB14 optimizer with a cosine schedule.}
elements.sent-2411-272: The learning rate is set to 2e-3, and the warmup rate is 0.03.
elements.sent-2411-273: For instruction tuning, the batch size is 32, and the learning rate is 2e-5.
elements.sent-2411-274: We empirically observe that training more than 1 epoch would hamper performance, we thus set the optimal training epoch to 1.
elements.sent-2411-275: All training and inference experiments were conducted under BF16 precision to save time and resources.

elements.sent-2411-278: It encompasses a diverse set of tasks aimed at long video comprehension and information extraction, which tests six core capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-279: Temporal Grounding (TG) focuses on understanding sequences and dynamics within the video.
elements.sent-2411-280: Summarization (Sum) requires an entire understanding of video from start to finish.
elements.sent-2411-281: Reasoning (Rea) involves four advanced reasoning skills: casual relationship identification, understanding for emotional development of character, understanding for underlying intentions of characters, future prediction.
elements.sent-2411-282: Entity Recognition (ER) requires the key entities tracking (such as people, places, and objects) throughout the video.
elements.sent-2411-283: Event Understanding (EU) needs to summarize the semantic concept for question answering.
elements.sent-2411-284: Key Information Retrieval (KIR) emphasizes retrieval of crucial detailed clues within videos.
